episode_title: Aliya Itzkowitz & Sam Gould (Part One): Insights from FT Strategies' AI Design Sprint Day

codes.description: Publishers feeling stuck or overwhelmed by the sheer number of AI possibilities, leading to inaction rather than experimentation
excerpts: there's so many possibilities that people tend to get a little bit sort of paralyzed almost by the the whole host of options that are out there
the goal of this day was really to get people unstuck outside of their own heads and really to move forward
codes.speaker: Aliya Itzkowitz

codes.description: The persistent gap between industry-wide discussion of AI and actual meaningful implementation by publishers
excerpts: as an industry, we've really been talking about AI for most of twenty twenty-three. But then it's only a few publishers that are really beginning to leverage it in a meaningful way and actually implement AI-driven solutions
codes.speaker: Aliya Itzkowitz

codes.description: Framing an AI strategy workshop around organizational mobilization rather than coding, focusing on blueprints, action plans, and ethics rather than building prototypes
excerpts: We were internally calling this day a non-technical hackathon because we weren't coding up this idea. But we were thinking about it more in terms of how do we mobilize the organization around this opportunity
codes.speaker: Sam Gould

codes.description: Deliberately starting AI exploration from business challenges and opportunities rather than from the technology itself
excerpts: we encouraged the participants to start by thinking about what opportunities and challenges are there in their business, then to think very broadly about how AI could be used to solve those challenges
we actually encouraged them very specifically to not think about AI, at least not too much
codes.speaker: Sam Gould

codes.description: Using iterative cycles of brainstorming broadly and then narrowing down to practical, strategically relevant ideas
excerpts: loosely based on a kind of Google design thinking, design sprint methodology. that kind of divergent and convergent thinking. So iterative steps of brainstorming, coming up with creative ideas, and then bringing that back to reality
codes.speaker: Sam Gould

codes.description: Intentionally bringing together representatives from across the business to break down departmental silos during AI design
excerpts: the day itself was a a very cross-functional endeavor. They brought representatives across their businesses. And that was a really kind of key principle of the day was to be collaborative, open, and really to break down silos across their businesses
having a diverse team of cross-functional representatives is a way to design more robust solutions and safer solutions
codes.speaker: Sam Gould

codes.description: Publishers overwhelmingly gravitating toward text-based generative AI use cases because text generation is top of mind, with relatively little multimedia or image-based exploration
excerpts: There's a clear trend right now where most of the ideas were more text-based because generative AI is so top of mind right now for people that a lot of the ideas focused on language-based. Ideas.
there was less multimedia interest. Probably because we tend to work more with print legacy publications
codes.speaker: Aliya Itzkowitz

codes.code: Content repackaging as dominant use case
codes.description: The most popular AI use case among publishers being reformatting existing stories into different formats (bullet points, shorter versions) for different audiences
excerpts: the most popular type of idea was something around the personalization or the reformatting of content. So we saw a lot of groups saying, how can we take an existing story, reformat it in a different way to serve the needs of different users?
those two ideas alone made up about 70% of the ideas in the room
codes.speaker: Aliya Itzkowitz

codes.description: Publishers envisioning AI performing traditional sub-editing functions like style guide application and error checking before publication
excerpts: the next biggest category was around sort of sub-editing or checking functions. So the role that a sub-editor would typically perform in the newsroom. How can we make sure that before this article's published we've applied our style guide or that we're just checking for basic errors
codes.speaker: Aliya Itzkowitz

codes.description: Separate publisher teams independently arriving at very similar AI use case ideas, suggesting industry-wide consensus on immediate AI opportunities
excerpts: all the teams that were there, the 20 or so teams that Sam mentioned, they were all working separately at their individual tables. And despite that, there was a lot of consensus around the ideas that people generated, which was really interesting to see
codes.speaker: Aliya Itzkowitz

codes.description: The necessity of grounding participants in AI fundamentals before productive strategy work can begin, including model mechanics, strategy, and ethics
excerpts: we really saw it as very important to educate these teams, educate these businesses before they really got got stuck in with thinking about AI and specific opportunities

Really a crash course in going from not really understanding AI, certainly being aware that it's a trend and feeling the need to grasp it, but not necessarily having had the time to really get into that field
codes.speaker: Sam Gould

codes.description: The importance of assigning specific individuals to own AI projects so they move beyond workshop enthusiasm into actual implementation
excerpts: I think it's too easy to come to a design workshop with loads of ideas and maybe not to stick your name or someone else's name on making that idea a reality
who would be responsible for that in their business?
codes.speaker: Sam Gould

codes.description: Publishers facing organizational demands to demonstrate concrete return on investment from AI initiatives, falling into either cost savings or revenue generation
excerpts: there's an interesting conversation that we are beginning to have now with a lot of publications around How can I make the case that this work I'm doing with AI is going to actually bring about ROI?

usually AI solutions are either saving you time. And therefore money, hopefully. Not always, but that's one group. And then the other group is AI is enabling innovation, which is potentially creating some new revenue stream
codes.speaker: Aliya Itzkowitz

codes.description: Self-selected AI enthusiasts within organizations needing to convince skeptical colleagues who may not see the potential
excerpts: the people that we work with are sort of self-selected. already interested in AI, already see the potential of AI. So now they need to go and convince the rest of their organizations who might be more skeptical
for some of the smaller companies, we basically only had in some cases one, maybe two people there working on the idea
codes.speaker: Aliya Itzkowitz

codes.description: Publishers at the design sprint leaning toward building custom AI solutions even when off-the-shelf tools exist, possibly influenced by the hands-on practical emphasis of the program
excerpts: More people were thinking in the build mindset, which was interesting for us to see, especially given some of the ideas weren't perhaps that complex. And some of the ideas are tools that exist out there in the market.
codes.speaker: Aliya Itzkowitz

codes.description: The principle that user needs must be validated through small proof of concepts before committing significant resources to AI development
excerpts: there should never be a big investment in some tool or some massive implementation if a user need in a very broad sense hasn't been validated
running those small proof of concepts is actually not too expensive
codes.speaker: Sam Gould

codes.description: Generative AI being significantly more accessible than previous AI approaches by eliminating expensive data labeling, infrastructure, and model training requirements
excerpts: generative AI is more accessible typically than AI of the past, in that you don't need an expensive process to go and label and clean thousands and thousands of data points and to spin up heavy infrastructure to train those models, but instead you can use a pre-trained LLM
codes.speaker: Sam Gould

codes.description: Reminding publishers that traditional machine learning and non-generative AI remain valuable for commercial and operational use cases, not just the text generation tools dominating headlines
excerpts: from the start of the whole process we emphasised that AI is broader than generative AI in the newsroom. It's also non-generative AI, machine learning, and and these slightly older models and can be applied in very commercial senses
codes.speaker: Sam Gould

codes.description: Publishers approaching AI from a defensive position shaped by historical trauma from previous technological disruptions rather than seeing opportunity
excerpts: often as an industry, we tend to approach new technologies from a position of loss aversion and thinking, how can we not lose? This is coming. How can we make sure it doesn't wreck our business model?
I think we have a lot of PTSD after so many rounds of forced transformations
codes.speaker: Aliya Itzkowitz

codes.description: Publisher attitudes shifting from fear to excitement over the course of a single day of structured AI engagement
excerpts: someone had written garbage tsunami at the beginning of the day. And then at the end of the day, they wrote opportunity tsunami
codes.speaker: Aliya Itzkowitz

codes.description: Deep concern among publishers about AI's impact on information quality, factual accuracy, and misinformation, particularly heading into election years
excerpts: in the industry that we operate in, there's a big responsibility on us in terms of the quality of the information, the validity of of those facts. And heading into an election year and a lot of big democracies next year, that's that's top of mind for people
codes.speaker: Aliya Itzkowitz

codes.description: Publisher employees worried about whether AI will make their jobs obsolete
excerpts: there's the side of job loss, what does this mean for my employees? Are people going to be out of a job?
codes.speaker: Aliya Itzkowitz

codes.description: Western publishers primarily concerned about intellectual property and content ownership while Asian publishers focus more on misinformation and fake news risks
excerpts: the narrative in the West is much more focused on this question of intellectual property and who who owns this content. Whereas in Asia, people are much more concerned around the issue of fake news and is this information correct
codes.speaker: Aliya Itzkowitz

codes.description: Infrastructure, data, and political challenges in the Global South limiting the ability to realize AI aspirations despite generative AI's increased accessibility
excerpts: although there is this accessibility of generative AI, there are still structural limitations, especially in the global south, as to how how easy it is to work with these technologies
There is there is a difference in what the aspirations are, but there's also a difference in how easy it is to realize those aspirations
codes.speaker: Sam Gould

codes.description: Smaller newsrooms being more agile and creative in AI adoption because they have fewer bureaucratic hoops, even if less resourced
excerpts: sometimes smaller newsrooms are a bit more scrappy. They have fewer hoops to jump through sometimes to actually get projects off the ground

we heard from this really tiny local newsroom in the UK that had found a way to build a model that helped them automate one of the key sections of their newspaper, which hilariously is to do with sheep auctions
codes.speaker: Aliya Itzkowitz

codes.description: Concern that in-house AI model building, being resource-intensive, will widen the gap between well-funded and under-resourced publishers
excerpts: for the building of in-house models, which is often very resource intensive. can be very expensive that I'm concerned that that might create division between haves and have nots
codes.speaker: Aliya Itzkowitz

codes.description: Top publications blocking AI crawlers inadvertently feeding lower-quality information into public AI models, harming the overall information ecosystem
excerpts: when a bunch of top publications ban Chat GPT from crawling their websites. I completely understand why they did that, but that also means that what's feeding those public models probably lower quality information
that goes back to the same problem that we have in the news industry in general with subscriptions where People self-select sort of unquote quality information
codes.speaker: Aliya Itzkowitz

codes.description: Embedding ethical considerations into the earliest stages of AI design rather than treating them as an afterthought, including human-in-the-loop as risk mitigation
excerpts: we always saw it as very important to bring AI ethics into the conversation as soon as possible
thinking about the importance of human in the loop as a risk mitigation and how to think practically about that
codes.speaker: Sam Gould

codes.description: Using unique datasets as the jumping-off point for AI use cases, rather than starting from process optimization or innovation alone
excerpts: are you in possession of some interesting data and that data set itself can be the jumping off point for your idea?
codes.speaker: Aliya Itzkowitz

codes.description: Thinking about people, technology, and processes needed to sustain AI initiatives beyond initial workshop excitement
excerpts: we encourage them to think about cap what we would call capabilities, so the people, the technology and the processes that need to be in place. To really sustain this initiative beyond the excitement of the design workshop
codes.speaker: Sam Gould

codes.description: Two distinct strategic approaches to AI: using it for cost reduction/efficiency versus using it as a product differentiator for user experience
excerpts: we also spoke about different business models and how to think, for example, about the differences between Using AI for efficiency as the main strategic goal versus using AI maybe as a product differentiator, some interesting features being added to your platforms
codes.speaker: Sam Gould

codes.description: The dangerous consequence of differential access to quality information creating parallel realities among different population segments
excerpts: People self-select sort of unquote quality information. People self-select whether or not they think it's worth paying for. And then you get a situation where people have different realities based on what they're reading. And that's quite dangerous
codes.speaker: Aliya Itzkowitz

codes.description: Developing economies producing surprisingly cutting-edge AI use cases despite structural limitations, like virtual avatars for sports reporting in the Philippines
excerpts: we are seeing some really, really exciting work coming out of places like the Philippines. There was a really interesting use case that we saw the other week with some generative AI, virtual avatars
codes.speaker: Sam Gould

codes.description: The significant range of AI maturity among publishers, from first-time explorers to those who have already deployed AI, all benefiting from structured engagement
excerpts: there was a real spectrum of AI experience in the room. So some of the publications, it was probably their first time really thinking hard about how to use AI
for other publications in the room, they'd certainly built and deployed AI in their businesses in the past
codes.speaker: Sam Gould

episode_summary: This episode reveals that the news industry's primary barrier to AI adoption is not technical but organizational and psychological: publishers are paralyzed by an abundance of options and a defensive mindset rooted in historical technology traumas. The convergence of 20 independent teams on nearly identical text-based use cases (content repackaging and sub-editing checks comprising 70% of ideas) suggests the industry is thinking narrowly about AI's potential, gravitating toward incremental efficiency gains rather than transformative innovation. The structured design sprint methodology exposed a critical insight: lowering barriers to experimentation through education, cross-functional collaboration, and deliberate problem-first thinking can shift publisher attitudes from 'garbage tsunami' to 'opportunity tsunami' in a single day. However, the discussion also surfaces a troubling paradox: the most resource-constrained publishers may be the most innovative adopters, yet the industry's defensive moves (like blocking AI crawlers) may degrade the very public information ecosystem they claim to protect.
surprises: Publishers independently arrived at nearly identical AI use cases despite working in isolation, suggesting the industry's imagination about AI is currently quite narrow and convergent
Workshop participants leaned toward building custom AI solutions even when off-the-shelf tools already existed, counter to expectations about resource-constrained organizations preferring to buy
A tiny UK local newsroom automated sheep auction reporting with a custom AI model, showing AI adoption is not just a big-publisher phenomenon
The attitude shift from 'garbage tsunami' to 'opportunity tsunami' happened in a single day, suggesting that hands-on structured engagement is remarkably effective at overcoming fear
Western publishers worry about IP while Asian publishers worry about misinformation, tied to different business model realities rather than different views on AI itself
tensions: GenAI is more accessible than ever, but structural barriers in the Global South mean this accessibility is unevenly distributed
Publishers blocking AI crawlers to protect their content simultaneously degrade the quality of public AI models that serve everyone, including their own audiences
The industry approaches AI defensively from PTSD, yet the structured workshop proved enthusiasm exists just beneath the fear
Small newsrooms are scrappier and more innovative, but in-house model building is resource-intensive and may widen the gap between AI haves and have-nots
Workshop participants need to demonstrate ROI to get organizational buy-in, but the most valuable AI experiments may be exploratory and hard to quantify upfront
